items.q: What is the main advantage of a CCD over the eye or photographic film?
items.correct: Its very high quantum efficiency, detecting most of the photons that reach it.
items.random_wrong: It removes the need for a telescope.
items.why: CCDs capture a large fraction of incoming light, far more efficiently than the eye or film.

items.q: What is dark current in a CCD?
items.correct: Unwanted signal from thermally generated electrons, reduced by cooling the detector.
items.misconception: Light pollution from the night sky.
items.why: Heat creates spurious electrons that mimic light, so detectors are cooled to suppress dark current.

items.q: Why do astronomers observe in many wavelengths (multi-wavelength astronomy)?
items.correct: Because different wavelengths reveal different physical processes and objects.
items.misconception: All wavelengths show exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Only visible light carries useful information.
items.why: Radio, infrared, X-ray and other bands expose features invisible to the eye, giving a fuller picture.

items.q: Which detector is a grid of pixels in silicon?
items.why: Correct answer: CCD. “PMT” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Single-photon timing. “Wide-field optical imaging” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximate maximum CCD quantum efficiency as a percentage?
items.why: Exact answer: 90. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
